title: Mangalorean Fish Curry

ingredients.name: firm white fish (seer fish or kingfish), curry cut

subs.name: 600 g prawns, shelled and deveined
subs.notes: reduce cooking time in step 5 to just 4–5 minutes

subs.name: 100 ml thick coconut milk added at step 4
subs.notes: gives a creamier, less grainy texture

ingredients.name: dried red chillies (Byadgi)
visual_checks: soak in water 15 minutes before grinding
ingredients.notes: soak in water 15 minutes before grinding

ingredients.name: kokum (dried), soaked in 3 tbsp warm water
visual_checks: this is the defining souring agent of Mangalorean fish curry; do not substitute with tamarind if possible
ingredients.notes: this is the defining souring agent of Mangalorean fish curry; do not substitute with tamarind if possible

subs.name: 1.5 tbsp tamarind pulp dissolved in 3 tbsp water
subs.notes: different flavour profile but a valid coastal substitute

subs.name: oil
subs.notes: coconut oil is strongly preferred for the coastal aroma

steps.text: Dry-roast the coriander seeds and cumin seeds together on a hot tawa for 1–2 minutes until they darken slightly and release their aroma. Cool briefly, then grind with the soaked dried red chillies, coconut, garlic, ginger and turmeric, adding 100 ml water, into a smooth, deep-red masala paste.
In a wide clay pot or heavy-bottomed pot, heat coconut oil on medium flame. Add the sliced onion and green chillies and fry for 4 minutes until the onion is soft and beginning to colour at the edges.
Add the ground masala paste to the pot and fry on medium heat for 3–4 minutes, stirring constantly, until the paste darkens by a shade and the oil starts to separate at the edges.

Pour in 300 ml water and bring to a gentle simmer. Add the kokum with its soaking water, black pepper and salt. The curry should taste pleasantly tart at this point — add more kokum or a dash of tamarind if it needs more sourness.

Gently slide in the fish pieces. Simmer on medium-low heat for 8–10 minutes without stirring — move the pot in circles instead of using a spoon, which would break the fish. The fish is done when it flakes easily at the thickest part.
Taste and adjust salt. Scatter coriander leaves on top. Rest for 10 minutes before serving — the curry develops its full depth after resting. Serve with steamed rice or neer dosa.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → 600 g prawns, shelled and deveined (reduce cooking time in step 5 to just 4–5 minutes); kokum → 1.5 tbsp tamarind pulp dissolved in 3 tbsp water (different flavour profile but a valid coastal substitute); coconut-oil → oil (coconut oil is strongly preferred for the coastal aroma); coconut → 100 ml thick coconut milk added at step 4 (gives a creamier, less grainy texture)
